chapters.title: Family bonds that outlast fame
chapters.text:
Nearly everyone knows Joe Biden — the forty-sixth U.S. president and longtime public figure. Far fewer recognize Valerie Biden Owens, even though she has been her brother's closest ally and adviser across his entire career.

Valerie was content to stand in Joe's shadow for most of her life. It was an unspoken pact that served them both. Their enduring partnership rested on two pillars: they never held grudges, and they shared the same worldview and values.

From childhood they spent countless hours together — playing, exploring, learning — until each became an expert on the other. In pivotal moments they often needed no words; trust and understanding filled the silence.

Every lasting relationship needs a foundation of shared morals and principles.

Close, deep bonds were among the few advantages the Bidens enjoyed growing up. They lacked wealth and status, but they had one another and an enduring sense of hope — a legacy that sustained their careers and passed to the next generation.

Valerie's story echoes many women's experiences. She grew up in a patriarchal mid-twentieth-century America, in a middle-class Irish Catholic household. As the only daughter among three brothers — Joe, Jimmy, and Frankie — she was raised to stand up for herself and for them. That family ethic later inspired Joe to offer Valerie a career path rarely open to women at the time. She would spend decades proving that the chair he pulled out was not a favor but a partnership.

I was the kid sister whose brother pulled up a chair for her at a table where there had been no room for women. ~ Valerie Biden Owens

chapters.title: The people around you shape who you become
chapters.text:
Valerie Biden was born November 5, 1945, to two hardworking parents. Her father was a strong man with even stronger convictions — the traditional head of household. Her mother was gentle, empathetic, and together they formed an exceptional parenting team that taught the Biden children how to live.

The family started in Scranton, Pennsylvania, then moved to Delaware seeking better work. The state became home and, years later, the launchpad for Joe's political career.

There the children internalized an unwritten code: tell the truth no matter what; stand up to bullies; treat everyone with dignity; never despair.

Those four rules guided their lives and later anchored Joe's political platform.

The Bidens function as a cohesive unit built on trust and love rather than rivalry. Whatever path a family member chose, the rest showed up to help without hesitation. When Joe, the eldest, entered politics, most of the family followed.

Excessive competition can destroy a relationship, so it's important to celebrate each other's successes and share the disappointments.

They began the political journey together and stood united when Joe was inaugurated as the forty-sixth president. Between that triumph and his first election as county councilman lay decades of joy and heartbreak, victory and loss. Through all of it, the Bidens remained a coalition. Their strength was never that they avoided conflict, but that they refused to let conflict become a permanent breach.

Our parents drilled it into our heads from birth that we were to take care of one another. In my mom's words: Family is the beginning, the middle, and the end. Period. ~ Valerie Biden Owens

chapters.title: Guarding your autonomy
chapters.text:
In fall 1959, Valerie enrolled at Ursuline Academy, a Catholic prep school for girls. Academically it gave her an excellent foundation; socially it was a struggle.

Coming from a household of three brothers, she was unprepared for an all-girls environment. She had always gravitated toward boyish pursuits and kept her classmates at a distance. During those years she wrestled with confidence and self-image. Joe, her older brother, stayed nearby, reminding her she was strong and beautiful.

By senior year, college and career planning loomed. Choosing a direction was difficult, but one conviction was firm: she wanted independence. Many women of the era were expected to marry well and devote themselves to family. Valerie wanted a career first. Later she made that the nonnegotiable rule for her daughters — pursue a profession and never depend entirely on a spouse.

Before committing to a relationship, ensure you have something to fall back on, as financial dependency traps too many women in unhappy or abusive relationships.

After deliberation, she entered the University of Delaware, where Joe was already a sophomore. Her grades earned her admission easily.

The siblings grew even closer. They were confidants and cheerleaders for each other. When Joe graduated and headed to Syracuse for law school in 1965, he still looked out for Valerie and even arranged a blind date with his friend Jack Owens — a disaster at the time, though years later she would recognize it as the day she met the love of her life.

Joe soon married Neilia Hunter, who quickly became family. She shared the Biden philosophy: family above all. Valerie's own romance was busy too — she dated Bruce Saunders, a fellow sophomore who later shipped off to Vietnam after graduation. College was a season of expansion for both siblings: new relationships, new ambitions, and the growing sense that their lives would remain intertwined no matter how far they traveled.

chapters.title: Breaking from the expected script
chapters.text:
During her junior year, Valerie took a short education course and immediately knew teaching was her calling. She had a gift for connecting with students and making everyone feel seen.

After graduation she accepted her first full-time job teaching eighth-grade social studies in Washington, D.C. — her first time living alone and far from family. It was frightening, but she welcomed the challenge. Growth, she believed, happened outside the comfort zone.

In 1968, after a rewarding year in D.C., she accepted a position at Wilmington Friends, a private school near home. The fit seemed perfect: strong students, engaged faculty, idealistic colleagues. A year in, she discovered she was paid far less than male teachers of comparable skill.

When she confronted the principal, he said an unmarried woman without children needed less money than men supporting families. The logic was patriarchal and common for the era, but no less shocking.

Constantly challenge and re-examine anything labeled as common sense. The current standards often shift and change depending on who dictates them.

In 1969, Bruce Saunders returned from Vietnam with an ultimatum: marry now or end the relationship. Saying yes felt automatic — everyone expected the next step. Despite nagging doubt, she went through with the wedding and missed the warning signs.

The marriage was not terrible, but it was not right either. Both were loyal and respectful, and for a time the relationship was balanced, if not destined to last. Valerie would later see the wedding as a lesson in listening to her own instincts instead of performing the script others expected from a young woman of her generation.

chapters.title: Courage to start something new
chapters.text:
In the late 1960s, Joe Biden returned to Delaware with a law degree and joined the public defender's office. Attorney Sid Balick encouraged him to run for an open seat on the New Castle County Council in 1970 — the first step into politics for Joe and for Valerie, whom he asked to manage the campaign.

They were unprepared and underfunded, but optimism, persistence, and belief in doing right carried them through. Door-to-door work and relentless effort won a small victory that set the rhythm for every campaign to follow.

With no money for advertising, Valerie enlisted her students at Friends School. They were captivated by Joe's passion and fresh ideas and became a grassroots force spreading word about an unknown candidate. Phone banks, door knocks, and handwritten flyers carried the campaign when television ads were out of reach.

As councilman, Joe became central to the Democratic Party's rebirth in Delaware. The party needed new faces as its values shifted, and insiders urged him to run for the U.S. Senate. It was a monumental leap for two siblings still learning the ropes, but neither could pass it up. Their first journey to Washington began.

90% of success is simply showing up and having the courage to rise to the challenge.

The Senate race defined both their lives. They upended conventional campaigning with bold choices and innovative ideas. Few believed in them, yet they had each other and their ideals — and they won. The victory announced a style they would repeat for decades: personal contact, moral clarity, and a refusal to let conventional wisdom dictate what was possible.

There's something exhilarating about being the underdog, and we embraced our freedom. ~ Valerie Biden Owens

chapters.title: How tragedy reshapes a family
chapters.text:
Just as Joe was preparing to take his Senate seat in 1972, catastrophe struck. His wife Neilia and their three children were in a devastating car crash. Neilia and their youngest daughter, Amy, died at the hospital. Sons Beau and Hunter were critically injured. The family's grief was unimaginable; Valerie still finds that night painful to discuss.

The accident nearly broke the Bidens, yet it also deepened their bonds.

Deep trauma influences our minds and memories in choosing what to bury in the subconsciousness and what to remember forever.

Valerie and her husband moved in with Joe to help care for Beau and Hunter. She left teaching and devoted herself to the boys. The family rallied to keep Joe going through the darkness, and he did not quit. Joe later said he considered stepping away from the Senate, but his family's insistence that he still had work to do helped pull him forward.

The crisis also tested Valerie's marriage to Bruce Saunders. They recognized it had run its course, parted amicably, and remained friends for life. What felt like failure at the time became the fresh start she needed.

Around the same time, Jack Owens — the failed blind date from college — reentered her life. As Joe's loyal friend, he dropped everything to help after the accident. Both wary of romance after past hurts, they slowly built trust. Honesty and the bond forged in crisis drew them together.

Their love was sudden, intense, and restorative. Jack helped Valerie climb out of depression and rediscover her own strength. Marriage to him would later give her a second family and a renewed belief that joy could return after devastation — not as denial of grief, but as proof that life could widen again.

chapters.title: Defending the American dream
chapters.text:
On October 11, 1975, Valerie became Valerie Biden Owens. She embraced the roles of wife and mother but never forgot the thrill of campaigning. When Joe's Senate reelection approached, she jumped back in.

Joe — among the youngest senators in history — served six terms, routinely winning more than 60 percent of the vote. Valerie managed every reelection campaign.

After thirty-six years in the Senate, the Biden coalition aimed higher. In January 2007, Joe and Valerie announced his candidacy for the 2008 presidential race. He lost the primary to Barack Obama, but Obama later invited Joe to join the ticket as vice president, valuing his deep experience. Joe never regretted saying yes.

Progress and success are never straightforward; great opportunities and outcomes might arise from what first appears to be a failure.

Joe spent eight years as vice president. For Valerie, that meant a break from constant campaigning. Though she loved the work and had become an expert, she never managed a campaign for anyone outside the family.

Instead she pursued new paths — work with Women's Campaign International and a fellowship at Harvard — sharing her knowledge and encouraging women to enter male-dominated fields. Those roles let her mentor a wider circle than any single campaign office could hold. She had managed winning races; now she wanted to widen the bench of women who could run their own.

Politics returned in 2019 when Joe ran for president again. They were confident because the campaign embodied the family's lifelong values. Running against Donald Trump, Joe framed the race as standing up to a bully and fighting for the nation's soul. For Valerie, the campaign was both familiar and historic — one more chance to defend the ideals their parents had drilled into them as children.

Did you know? More people voted in the 2019 presidential election than ever in U.S. history.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
When Joe Biden was sworn in as the forty-sixth president, the victory belonged to the whole family — perhaps most of all to Valerie. In 1970 she bet on her brother and poured her energy into his political life. Watching him in the Oval Office felt like the satisfying culmination of both their journeys.

Valerie's partnership with Joe is a remarkable case study in determination and teamwork. Her life mixed adventure with hardship — crushing blows alongside good fortune. Her perseverance through it all is inspiring. She managed campaigns, raised children, rebuilt after divorce, and still found new ways to serve beyond her brother's shadow.

Her story is also one of courage. She never hesitated to make hard calls or plunge into unknown territory, largely because family was her anchor. Throughout their lives, the Bidens were one another's true north; caring for family was woven into their DNA.

Empathy guided every major decision Valerie made. Her deepest lesson is simple: small acts of kindness can transform and heal a person — and perhaps a nation.

Kindness reverberates. What you say and do matters. That is what I learned — growing up Biden. ~ Valerie Biden Owens

Try this

• Focus on the relationships within your family and think about how they compare to the Bidens' dynamic. Then, use this opportunity to show some extra appreciation to your family members.
• Follow Valerie Biden Owens on social media to keep up with her journey and learn more about her current work.
• You can also read a summary of Joe Biden's autobiography, Promises to Keep, on the Headway app to get another perspective on the Bidens' story.
